dependencies.rationale: The requirement to use the Horizon system arose from the subpostmasters' contractual relationship with the Defendant; P0001 is the foundational premise.
The proposition that apparent shortfalls did not reflect actual losses is logically premised on the system containing defects capable of generating such artificial shortfalls.
The wrongfulness of requiring subpostmasters to make good shortfalls is premised on those shortfalls having been caused by the Horizon system rather than genuine losses.
Requiring payment of shortfalls that did not reflect real losses depends on establishing that the shortfalls were not genuine.
The Defendant's requirement that subpostmasters make good discrepancies was grounded in its representation that such discrepancies were the subpostmaster's responsibility.
The allegation that the Defendant pursued or prosecuted subpostmasters for Horizon-error shortfalls presupposes that those shortfalls were the product of system defects.
Prosecuting subpostmasters for shortfalls not caused by their dishonesty or negligence is premised on those shortfalls not reflecting actual losses attributable to the subpostmaster.
A duty to disclose known faults, and a breach of that duty, can only arise if the Defendant first had knowledge of those faults; P0007 is the necessary premise.
The financial loss suffered by Claimants flows as a consequence from being required to make good shortfalls caused by Horizon rather than by themselves.
Damage to reputation and distress flows as a consequence from the Defendant's pursuit and prosecution of subpostmasters for Horizon-generated shortfalls.
Loss suffered by Claimants is partly a consequence of the Defendant's failure to investigate reported discrepancies, which would have identified the system as the cause.
Loss suffered by Claimants is partly a consequence of the Defendant's failure to disclose known faults, which left subpostmasters without information to resist wrongful demands.

The Claimants' argument that the contractual allocation of responsibility did not extend to system-caused losses is logically premised on establishing that the Horizon system did in fact cause those losses.
The Defendant's assertion of contractual responsibility depends on the existence of the contracts between the parties, which is the foundational premise admitted in P0001.
The denial that remote access was used improperly is logically built upon the admission in P0017 that such remote access capability existed; P0017 is the necessary premise.
The denial that Claimants suffered loss as alleged is premised on the Defendant's position that the Horizon system was not defective in any material respect.
The denial of loss is also premised on the assertion that the shortfalls shown by Horizon reflected genuine losses for which the subpostmasters were properly liable.
